scenario: Mastodon work session - audit the Status contract before a teardown rework

map_quality.rationale: Identifies Mastodon as a Rails 8/ActivityPub server, names all key directories (models, services, workers, lib, policies, serializers, chewy), pins the central model at app/models/status.rb:34, and calls out FeedManager/StatusFilter/StatusReachFinder/ActivityPub::TagManager in lib/ — enough for small targeted lookups. Minor gap: the lib/ helpers and concerns subdirs lack exact file paths.

specificity.rationale: app/models/status.rb:34 is concrete and correct; workers/activitypub/ and app/models/concerns/status/* are named as paths. However FeedManager, StatusFilter, StatusReachFinder, and ActivityPub::TagManager appear only as bare names under 'lib/' with no file:line, which forces one follow-up per helper.

justification.rationale: The answer states Status 'is the hub feeds, notifications, federation, moderation, search and trends all reference' — that is the right why — but does not explain why behavior is split across concerns (reuse across models), why services exist instead of fat-model methods, or why federation delivery is async workers rather than inline.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere; the answer reads as fully confident despite covering a large codebase in 300s. The rubric asks for flagging where connections are unclear — none are acknowledged.

relationship.rationale: The answer says feeds/notifications/federation/moderation 'all reference' Status, but states only the endpoint, not the connecting path: it does not say FeedManager reads status IDs from Redis, or that federation wraps statuses in ActivityPub objects via ActivityPub::TagManager, or which concerns inject what behavior into Status — a text search could produce the same endpoint list.

steps.step: Map the Status contract and what it is built on

map_quality.rationale: The answer hands the agent a near-complete map: model at app/models/status.rb:34, every concern with file:line, every association with line and dependent strategy, and all teardown-critical callbacks (counter caches, webhook workers, Chewy reindex, discard_with_reblogs). An agent could proceed with only small, targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every concern is named by exact file (app/models/concerns/status/visibility.rb:3, threading_concern.rb:3, etc.) with specific line numbers for the methods they supply; associations are enumerated with line citations (`:77` through `:118`).

justification.rationale: The answer explains what each piece does for an agent changing the contract ('This is the single most load-bearing fact for a teardown rework', 'must run before dependent: :destroy wipes mentions', 'pervasive across dependents') but does not explain WHY behavior was split into this concern structure architecturally — it stays at the what-matters-for-change level rather than the why-designed-this-way level.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents every detail as confirmed fact with no caveats, no 'I could not verify', and no flags on parts that might differ at runtime or under migrations — uncertainty is entirely absent.

relationship.rationale: For each concern the answer states the specific binding: SafeReblogInsert 'overrides _insert_record to block reblogs of discarded statuses at the DB layer', ThreadingConcern supplies 'recursive CTEs', Visibility supplies 'the visibility enum + distributable?/hidden? + visibility scopes'; associations carry dependent strategies (nullify/destroy/delete) that are the relationship contract. This goes well beyond a file list.

steps.step: Trace how a status is created and distributed

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a fully ordered, five-layer trace (entry/persist → model callbacks → postprocess dispatch → local fan-out → federation) with file:line at every hand-off and an explicit inline-vs-async summary; an agent can act on this with only small confirmation lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every component is named exactly with file:line — `PostStatusService#call app/services/post_status_service.rb:42`, `FanOutOnWriteService app/services/fan_out_on_write_service.rb:12`, `FeedManager#push_to_home app/lib/feed_manager.rb:76` — leaving nothing vague.

justification.rationale: The answer correctly labels each step inline or async but never states WHY distribution is pushed to workers (fan-out scale, avoiding request blocking); the motivation is implicit at best.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags async boundaries clearly throughout but does not acknowledge any part of the chain it could not fully open or verify (e.g., the internals of `ActivityPub::DeliveryWorker` or notification branching inside `FanOutOnWriteService`).

relationship.rationale: Creation is assembled as an explicit numbered chain — PostStatusService → model callbacks → postprocess_status! → DistributionWorker → FanOutOnWriteService → FeedInsertWorker → FeedManager, and a parallel fork to ActivityPub::DistributionWorker → StatusReachFinder → ActivityPub::DeliveryWorker — with each hop's connecting call named.

steps.step: Audit every dependent of the Status contract

map_quality.rationale: Nine grouped sections cover feeds, federation, notifications, moderation, account lifecycle, import/export/backup, background maintenance, direct-association models, and the serialization surface — a genuinely exhaustive sweep with file:line throughout. A handful of rubric-called-out items are absent (app/lib/permalink_redirector.rb, an email-distribution worker, and the 'link feed' redis path), but the breadth is far above what a text search would produce.

specificity.rationale: The vast majority of entries carry concrete file:line and a one-phrase description of the status dependency (e.g., 'status_reach_finder.rb:3 — computes delivery inboxes... reads reblog?, quote, mentions, reblogs, deleted_at (:70)'). Section H collapses the direct-association models into a flat list of filenames with no line numbers or per-model phrases, which is the main specificity gap.

justification.rationale: Grouping by area (A–I) supplies the architectural WHY for each cluster, and individual entries name the mechanism ('bulk delete via delete_all (skips callbacks), manual conversation unlink', 'per-account auto-deletion query builder'). The answer explains grouping rationale but does not explain the deeper architectural reason Status is the hub (e.g., why trends, backup, and annual reports all derive from statuses rather than accounts).

uncertainty.rationale: The answer makes no explicit acknowledgement that a static pass can miss dependents reached through concerns, polymorphic associations, or dynamic dispatch — despite the step prompt calling this out as a key risk. The polymorphic notification.rb entry is noted structurally but no uncertainty flag is raised about what this static enumeration might still omit.

relationship.rationale: For most dependents the connecting path is stated: 'belongs_to :activity polymorphic; status.rb:114 has_one :notification, as: :activity', 'status_ids → Status.with_discarded', 'has_many :statuses (account/associations.rb:40)', and backup_service's derive route through ActivityPub serializers. Section H lists association models by filename only with no per-entry relationship clause, and the missing permalink_redirector leaves one non-obvious path unconnected.

steps.step: Trace how status identity and visibility are guarded

map_quality.rationale: The answer maps existence guards (default_scope, with_discarded opt-outs, RecordNotFound rescues, check_race_condition!) and visibility gates (StatusPolicy#show?, FeedManager#filter, StatusReachFinder#distributable?, FanOutOnWriteService#broadcastable?) with file:line throughout, covering feeds, federation, moderation, and threading paths; the backup path visibility checks are not addressed, leaving a small gap.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every guard is cited with exact method name and line: `default_scope { recent.kept }` at status.rb:128, `check_race_condition!` at fan_out_on_write_service.rb:29, `filtered?` at status_filter.rb:11, `show?` at status_policy.rb:4 — the level of precision is high across both sections.

justification.rationale: The answer explains WHAT each guard does ("no-op on a vanished status", "DB-level guarantee", "teardown work must still see dead rows") but never states the WHY that spans them all: a status may be deleted, suspended, or its visibility changed between when async work was enqueued and when it runs, requiring each path to re-confirm state.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no missing guards, implicit checks, or areas it could not confirm; there is no acknowledgment of paths (e.g., backup export) where guard coverage is uncertain or absent.

relationship.rationale: The answer connects several guards to their protecting context (StatusFilter → StatusPolicy#show? at :70, FeedManager#filter before insertion, StatusReachFinder gating federated deletes), but stops short of assembling explicit chains showing how each guard sits on the path between a caller and the status (e.g., FanOutOnWriteService → FeedManager#filter → filter_from_home → distributable? as a full chain); relationships are implied rather than stated.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers feeds, federation, moderation, account lifecycle, backup, search/trends, and webhooks with a tiered risk ranking (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM) and file:line throughout; the 'must re-verify' closing section is directly actionable. Notifications as a standalone area and background maintenance workers are thin, but the breadth across the other areas and the explicit ranking of what 'pages someone' (SafeReblogInsert) gives an agent a strong starting map.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every dependent is named with a concrete file and line — `safe_reblog_insert.rb:20`, `batched_remove_status_service.rb:34`, `delete_account_service.rb:160-205`, `remove_status_service.rb:92`, `feed_manager.rb:90,117`, `status_policy.rb:4` — with a one-phrase reason for the risk level, leaving very little the agent needs to rediscover.

justification.rationale: Each tier has a stated consequence: the kept default scope is a codebase-wide silent landmine; SafeReblogInsert breaks reblog integrity at the DB layer; BatchedRemoveStatusService bypassing dependent: options means rows leak unless mirrored in DeleteAccountService; federation deletes missing means ghost posts on remote servers. The WHY is concrete and failure-mode-grounded, not just structural.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer contains no explicit 'I am not certain about X' flags; the closest it gets is 'any new dependent or changed dependent: option must be mirrored' (implied gap) and the catch-all 'must re-verify' section, but these read as instructions rather than acknowledged unknowns. An agent reading this would not know which of the MEDIUM items the answer is least confident about.

relationship.rationale: Most dependents are tied to their connecting relationship — SafeReblogInsert via raw SQL on `deleted_at`, the teardown graph via named `dependent: :destroy/:nullify/:delete` options, BatchedRemoveStatusService via explicit `delete_all` callback bypass, FASP via `after_commit` hooks, FeedManager via `unpush` — not merely listed as files. A few MEDIUM entries (backup_service, account_statuses_cleanup_policy) lack the connecting path, but the CRITICAL and HIGH items are well-connected.

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a complete map across all eight areas: edit targets with line ranges (`status.rb:66,128,89-118,400,406`), every dependent grouped by area (feeds, federation, notifications, moderation, account lifecycle, backup, maintenance, dependent models), specs by path, and fabricators named explicitly. The JSON section reinforces with a structured `change_verification_map` block. A teammate could land the change from this map alone with only small, targeted follow-ups.

specificity.rationale: Every entry carries a concrete file path and line reference — `safe_reblog_insert.rb:20-75`, `delete_account_service.rb:160-205`, `trends/statuses.rb:33/45/63` — and the spec section distinguishes Fabrication fabricators from FactoryBot by name and glob. No vague 'the services layer' language survives.

justification.rationale: The `blast_radius_ranked` section explicitly explains WHY each item is critical/high/medium (e.g., 'alters every unscoped query; all with_discarded sites must be re-verified', 'bypasses callbacks'). The edit list embeds purpose in parentheticals, but the dependent-review list is mostly a grouped enumeration without a per-file why — the justification is concentrated in the blast section rather than distributed across all three map sections.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the full map with uniform confidence and never flags any area as 'could not confirm' or 'static pass may miss'. The risk ranking implicitly grades certainty, but there is no explicit acknowledgment that dynamic-dispatch paths or concern-reached dependents may be incomplete.

relationship.rationale: Most entries carry a parenthetical connecting relationship: `notification.rb (status.rb:114 polymorphic destroy)`, `report.rb:78 (with_discarded :79)`, `batched_remove_status_service.rb:10 (delete_all :34, unlink :28, deindex :38)`, and the concerns block maps each concern to the exact behavior it contributes. Non-obvious feed/backup/moderation dependents include the mechanism, not just the file. A handful of entries in the 'review/touch' prose section list files without an explicit path, but these are the minority.
